top_strategic.title: Brand Safety API for Sports Streaming
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Sell real-time brand classification as a moderation layer to streaming platforms (YouTube, Twitch, Facebook Gaming). They need to flag unlicensed brand placement, sports betting logos, and competitor products in live feeds. Our taxonomy + rules become the standard enforcement layer. As more platforms adopt it, our rule set gets battle-tested across sports.

counterargument.argument: Selling brand classification as a 'moderation layer' to YouTube/Twitch requires real-time SLA guarantees, legal liability for false flags, and competitor-proof accuracy you haven't validated at scale outside pickleball.
counterargument.risk: If your classifier flags a legitimate brand as 'unlicensed' and the platform acts on it (demonetizes, blocks, or mutes), you expose the brand owner and platform to legal liability. You'll also fragment your taxonomy across customer-specific customizations, making it unmaintainable.

top_strategic.title: Reverse Brand Intelligence (Competitive Exposure Monitoring)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Sell to brands (Selkirk, Wilson, Yonex) a private dashboard showing every competitor mention in pickleball content—paddles in hand, apparel worn, signage behind players. Invert the model: instead of 'what brands appear,' ask 'where do MY competitors appear?' Monthly subscription per brand, feeds their marketing and partnership teams.

counterargument.argument: A 'competitive exposure' dashboard requires you to classify *every brand mention with perfect precision*—but your unknown rate is 564 detections (Finding #7), which is 7.5% of your corpus.
counterargument.risk: If you sell this to Selkirk as 'every competitor mention,' but your classifier misses 7-10% of actual mentions or falsely flags non-competitors, you'll erode trust with the first paying customer. You'll also create contractual liability—they're making business decisions on your data.

top_strategic.title: Sport Taxonomy Factory (Whitelabel Rule Engine)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Package the 49-rule decision framework + taxonomy-building process as a managed service. Beach volleyball league uploads 500 clips → we deliver a 45-rule classifier for their sport in 6 weeks. Recurring revenue from 10-15 niche sports (padel, kabaddi, lacrosse, aussie rules). Each sport's rules feed into a master taxonomy that makes the next sport faster and cheaper.

counterargument.argument: A 'whitelabel rule engine' sounds like a revenue play, but beach volleyball has zero overlap with pickleball taxonomy, so you're selling a generic tool, not a proven framework.
counterargument.risk: If you package this as a spinoff, you're committing to maintain *two* taxonomies (pickleball + customers), and any bug in the shared framework breaks both. You'll also lose focus on pickleball core product while debugging customer-specific edge cases. Early customers will reveal that your 49-rule framework doesn't transfer.

top_strategic.title: Player-Brand Affinity Scoring (Athlete Marketplace)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Build a 'Brand Fit Score' for every pro/semi-pro player: which brands appear in their footage, how frequently, in what context? Sell this to brands as athlete scouting data for sponsorship deals ('Selkirk has 73% affinity overlap with demographics buying $300+ paddles'). Becomes a labor marketplace: players get rated, brands get matched, our classifier is the matching engine.

top_strategic.title: Venue Sponsorship Optimization Algorithm
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Venues want to know: 'If we sign a sponsorship with Brand X, how much on-camera exposure will they actually get?' Feed our classifier into a simulator that predicts brand visibility for any venue based on tournament size, camera angles, player roster. Venues pay $500/month for the tool, brands pay $2k to model different sponsorship scenarios. Classifier is the engine.

top_strategic.title: Equipment Authenticity Verifier (Anti-Counterfeiting)
top_strategic.evidence_summary: Brands fear fake equipment flooding the market. Use the classifier to create a 'brand passport' service: when a player registers gear, our system learns the exact wear patterns, logos, color codes. Later, if that paddle appears in unauthorized footage or marketplaces, flag it. Brands pay per-athlete-per-season subscription. Creates a moat: only we have the training corpus.

top_data.title: Top 5 rules handle 91.1% of all classifications — heavy concentration risk
top_data.evidence_summary: Top 5: JOOLA(5820), Nike(591), PICKLEBALL(196), Reolink(109), HEAD(103). If any of these rules has a bug, it cascades across 91.1% of the corpus. Total rule-based classifications: 7489.
top_data.recommended_action: Add regression tests for top 5 rules; consider sub-rules for edge cases

counterargument.argument: Concentration risk is real, but regression tests alone won't catch silent semantic drift—a rule can pass regression tests and still emit false positives at higher confidence thresholds downstream.
counterargument.risk: If you add regression tests but don't audit the *definition* of those top 5 rules (especially JOOLA at 5820 fires—is that one rule or five rules bundled?), you're making the codebase look safer while the underlying rules accumulate technical debt. A 'sub-rule' refactor could cascade failures across dashboards if dependent systems expect flat rule IDs.

top_data.title: 5 rules are NOT human-verified but actively classify 28 detections
top_data.evidence_summary: Unverified: THE UNDERGROUND PICKLEBALL, Wilson, THE RECOVERY CAVE, BODYARMOR, COMCAST BUSINESS. These were auto-generated or batch-approved without individual review. They fire 28 times — if wrong, they silently corrupt the corpus.
top_data.recommended_action: Review and verify unverified rules, prioritized by fire count

counterargument.argument: 28 detections from unverified rules is small enough that you could manually verify them today—this is already actionable and likely has high ROI.
counterargument.risk: This finding is solid, but the *order of operations* matters: if you verify these 5 rules in isolation and don't trace back to ask 'how many other rules were batch-approved without review?', you're treating the symptom, not the disease. You may have 20 more lurking.

top_data.title: 6 unknown brands appear 5+ times — probably real and need rules
top_data.evidence_summary: Rescuable unknowns: JOOLA(435), goomba(21), R&M(20), Foz_4_UG_Court(14), R&M_fox_wifi_goomba(9), R8M(8). These are classified 'unknown' but appear consistently enough to be real brands. Total unknown detections: 564.
top_data.recommended_action: Research and create rules for top unknown brands

counterargument.argument: Unknown detections at 5+ occurrences is a reasonable heuristic, but JOOLA (435 fires) is already in your top 5 rules (Finding #3)—this suggests your 'unknown' bucket is contaminated by unmapped variations of known brands.
counterargument.risk: Creating new rules for 'unknowns' without first auditing *why* JOOLA fires 435 times but also appears 435 times in the unknown bucket will lead to rule duplication and silent conflicts. You'll also inherit whatever classification errors are already in that 435-fire subset.

top_data.title: 15 brands appear 3+ times with NO rule — pure fallback classification
top_data.evidence_summary: Rule-less brands: Foz_Bullet(62), R&M_foz_wifi_goomba(40), N(40), 4K(20), ODESSA(18), PSA(17), Nicol(14), PKLR(12), W(10), COACH(8). These are classified by heuristic only — no human-verified category assignment. Total fallback-only detections: 272.
top_data.recommended_action: Create rules for top fallback-only brands, starting with highest count

top_data.title: Revenue potential predicts approval: high=80.0%, low=27.3%, none=52.4%
top_data.evidence_summary: Approval rates by revenue bucket: {'none': 52.4, 'medium': 38.1, 'high': 80.0, 'low': 27.3}. High-revenue brands get approved more often — the system could auto-approve them.
top_data.recommended_action: Consider auto-approving high-revenue brand rules with >80% historical approval rate

top_data.title: 4 junk names pollute the unknown bucket — dates, gibberish, single chars
top_data.evidence_summary: Junk entries: 7(26), UG(13), Unknown(9), 2026-01-05(5). These are Gemini hallucinations or OCR artifacts (dates, single characters). They waste investigation effort by appearing in the unknown queue.
top_data.recommended_action: Add a pre-filter to catch junk patterns before classification

top_data.title: 12 rules exist purely to suppress noise — they fire 338 times
top_data.evidence_summary: Noise-suppressor rules (revenue_potential=none): Courtana, PICKLEBALL, 7, goomba, R&M, Unknown, UG, R&M_fox_wifi_goomba. These classify junk brands like dates, gibberish, venue artifacts. Intel rules (37) fire 7129x — ratio: 21.1:1 intel-to-noise.
top_data.recommended_action: Consider a 'junk bucket' rule instead of per-brand noise rules

top_data.title: 15 proposed rules pending review — 13 need investigation
top_data.evidence_summary: Proposed: ODESSA, PSA, PKLR, FANADIR, READY TO PLAY, Fila, VAERSA, DUDE. These have been auto-generated but not approved. Every day they sit, their detections stay unclassified.
top_data.recommended_action: Review proposed rules in classifier morning report

top_data.title: Decision distribution: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3} across 74 decisions
top_data.evidence_summary: Action breakdown: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3}. Unique brands decided on: 50. This shows how heavily the system relies on Bill's input.
top_data.recommended_action: Track decision velocity — are decisions keeping pace with proposals?

top_data.title: 9 brands have been sent to investigation 2+ times — decision limbo
top_data.evidence_summary: Repeat investigations: R&M_foz_wifi_goomba, W, PKLR, PBS, PSA, FANADIR, ODESSA, VAERSA, WD. These brands keep bouncing between proposed and investigate without resolution. Each loop wastes a classifier cycle.
top_data.recommended_action: Force-decide repeat investigations: approve with caveats or reject definitively

all_findings.title: Category overlap: headwear consolidation — 2 near-synonyms hold 2 detections
all_findings.evidence_summary: Categories: {'headwear': 1, 'hat': 1}. These could be merged into one canonical category. Fragmentation makes aggregate reports unreliable (e.g. 'how many apparel detections?' requires summing 6 categories).
all_findings.recommended_action: Merge into single canonical category with sub-type field

all_findings.title: 15 brands appear 3+ times with NO rule — pure fallback classification
all_findings.evidence_summary: Rule-less brands: Foz_Bullet(62), R&M_foz_wifi_goomba(40), N(40), 4K(20), ODESSA(18), PSA(17), Nicol(14), PKLR(12), W(10), COACH(8). These are classified by heuristic only — no human-verified category assignment. Total fallback-only detections: 272.

all_findings.recommended_action: Review proposed rules in classifier morning report

all_findings.title: 2 clips have WEAK contamination signals — worth a spot check
all_findings.evidence_summary: Borderline clips: 'Friends play a casual game of ping pong in a garag' (score=0.85); 'Two players engage in a consistent kitchen volley ' (score=0.7)
all_findings.recommended_action: Spot-check medium-confidence clips when reviewing morning report

medium_suspects.summary: Friends play a casual game of ping pong in a garage, exchanging drives and volleys.
Two players engage in a consistent kitchen volley rally.

all_findings.title: 4 clips quarantined so far — {'non_pickleball_sport': 4} breakdown
all_findings.evidence_summary: Current quarantine: 4 clips. Reasons: {'non_pickleball_sport': 4}. Pipeline catch rate: 0.2% of corpus. This is healthy.
all_findings.recommended_action: No action needed — quarantine working as expected

counterargument.argument: A 0.2% catch rate on only 4 clips is statistically meaningless—you haven't seen enough false negatives to claim the quarantine is 'healthy.'
counterargument.risk: Acting on 'no action needed' based on n=4 masks the real problem: you may have zero visibility into non-pickleball sport contamination that slipped through. When the corpus grows 10x, you'll discover systematic misses that have been silently corrupting downstream reports for months.

all_findings.title: Non-pickleball sport mentions in summaries: {'squash': 2}
all_findings.evidence_summary: Across 3513 clips: {'pickleball': 645, 'squash': 2}. Pickleball mentioned 645x. Other sports: {'squash': 2}. Some may be false positives (e.g. 'tennis background' or comparing styles).
all_findings.recommended_action: Cross-reference non-pickleball mentions with contamination scores

all_findings.title: Decision distribution: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3} across 74 decisions
all_findings.evidence_summary: Action breakdown: {'approve': 33, 'investigate': 38, 'reject': 3}. Unique brands decided on: 50. This shows how heavily the system relies on Bill's input.
all_findings.recommended_action: Track decision velocity — are decisions keeping pace with proposals?

all_findings.evidence_summary: Approval rates by revenue bucket: {'none': 52.4, 'medium': 38.1, 'high': 80.0, 'low': 27.3}. High-revenue brands get approved more often — the system could auto-approve them.

all_findings.title: 9 brands have been sent to investigation 2+ times — decision limbo
all_findings.evidence_summary: Repeat investigations: R&M_foz_wifi_goomba, W, PKLR, PBS, PSA, FANADIR, ODESSA, VAERSA, WD. These brands keep bouncing between proposed and investigate without resolution. Each loop wastes a classifier cycle.
all_findings.recommended_action: Force-decide repeat investigations: approve with caveats or reject definitively

all_findings.title: Backlog ratio: 15 pending vs 74 decided = 0.2x
all_findings.evidence_summary: There are 15 rules pending review and 74 total decisions made. Backlog is manageable.
all_findings.recommended_action: Backlog under control — no action needed

counterargument.argument: A 0.2x backlog ratio says nothing about *age* of pending rules—if some have been pending for 6+ months, you have a process breakdown, not a backlog problem.
counterargument.risk: Declaring 'backlog under control' without audit trail dates creates false confidence. A rule pending for 200 days looks the same as one pending for 5 days in this metric. You may have zombie rules blocking taxonomy evolution while you think the pipeline is healthy.

all_findings.title: Pickleball Brand Stock Exchange (Exposure Trading)
all_findings.evidence_summary: Create a marketplace where brands bid for 'exposure minutes' in upcoming tournament footage. Classifier predicts which players/venues will be filmed and lets brands compete to be featured. Becomes a revenue share play with tournament organizers (they get 30%, we take 20%, brands pay premium for guaranteed classified visibility). Classifier becomes the source of truth for valuation.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: media_play play

all_findings.title: Crowdsourced Rule Refinement Bounty Program
all_findings.evidence_summary: Open a 'Rule Board' where independent video annotators worldwide compete to improve classification accuracy. Pay $50-500 per validated edge case that breaks or improves a rule. As the corpus scales to 50k+ clips, crowd gets smarter, rules get bulletproof, and we own the strongest taxonomy in sports. Double down on our moat.
all_findings.recommended_action: Evaluate: crowdsource play
